Facts.text: A temple dedicated to Venus Erycina is built on Capitoline Hill to commemorate the Roman defeat at Lake Trasimene
Damascus surrenders to the Nūr al-Dīn, ruler of Aleppo
St. George's Night Uprising in Estonia
Battle of Villalar: Spanish King Charles I defeats the Comuneros rebels and captures three of their leaders, who are executed the following day
Dutch Fifth Expedition sets sail from the Dutch Republic for Sumatra, and the Moluccas to trade in spices
Netherlands takes control of De Briel, Vlissingen and Fort Rammekens from England
Count of Nassau-Dietz, Ernst Casimir I, is appointed Stadtholder of Groningen and Drenthe
The Heilbronn League formed by Sweden and Protestant German monarchy in the Free Imperial City of Heilbronn
Oldest US public institution, Boston Latin School founded
Treaty of Oliva is established between Sweden and Poland, helping to end the Second Northern War
Warren Hastings, Governor-General of India, acquitted in England of high treason
Missolonghi, Greece captured by Turks
Irish mathematician and astronomer William Rowan Hamilton presents his Theory of Systems of Rays
English steamship "Great Western" crosses the Atlantic docks in NYC
Canada issues its first postage stamp, a Three-Pence Beaver
Democratic convention in Charleston, South Carolina divided over slavery
Arkansas troops seize Fort Smith on the Arkansas River
Battle of Cane River, Louisiana (Red River Expedition, Monett's Ferry)
1st professional baseball exhibition game - Cincinnati Red Stockings 24, Cincinnati amateurs  15
Blossom Rock in San Francisco Bay blown up
John Heemskerk Abrahamszoon forms Dutch government
Jews are expelled from Moscow, Russia
Vitascope system of movie projection 1st shown at Koster & Bial's Music Hall, New York City
First known occurrence of the word "hillbilly" (NY Journal)
NY Highlanders (Yankees) win their first game; beat Washington Senators, 7-2 at American League Park
American Academy of Arts & Letters forms
Denmark, Germany, Britain, France, Netherlands & Sweden sign North Sea accord
International Exhibition opens in Brussels, Belgium
MLB Chicago Federals host the Kansas City Packers in the 1st game played at Weeghman Park (now Wrigley Field)
Lord Dunsany's "Night at an Inn" premieres in NYC
Raid of Zeebrugge; the British navy attempts to block German vessels from leaving port by sinking obsolete ships - mostly fails
Zeebrugge Raid: Dover Patrol attempted to block Belgian port of Bruges-Zeebrugge to prevent German U-boats from leaving port
US Major League Baseball opens a reduced 140-game season
Charley Paddock runs a world record 100m in 10.4 seconds
Inauguration ceremonies take place at Gdynia, Poland to mark its new status as a temporary military port and fishers' shelter
British Empire Exhibition opens at Wembley, London
Having badly defeated Spain and driven her out of Spanish Morocco, the native Riffi, led by Abd-el-Krim, turn on the French in French Morocco
Pastor LH Perquin forms Union of Catholic Dutch Radio (KRO)
153-year old De Adriaan Windmill in Haarlem, the Netherlands, burns down
ShakespeareMemorial Theatre opens at Stratford-on-Avon, England
Polish Constitution of 1935 is adopted
Dance hall fires kills 198 in Natchez, Mississippi
Greek Army surrenders to Germany; British RAF evacuates the Greek King George II to Egypt
1st night Exeter bombed by German Luftwaffe
4-day allied bombing on Rostock, Germany begins
British & US offensive directed at Tunis and Bizerta
Flossenbürg concentration camp liberated by US army troops
US troops in Italy cross river Po
Brooklyn Dodger Ed Head no-hits Boston Braves, 5-0
Minnesota's first television station, KSTP TV channel 5 (ABC affiliate), aired its 1st broadcast
Chinese Red army conquers Nanjing
Courtesy mail boxes for motorists started in San Francisco
Netherlands annex several German territories, including Elten and Tuddern, as apart of post-Wold War II border adjustments
1st Major League Baseball day game completed under lights (Phillies 6, Braves 5)
Nationalist Chinese forces (Kuomintang) evacuates Hainan Island after being defeated by the People's Liberation Army
Stanley Cup Final, Olympia Stadium, Detroit, MI: Pete Babando scores Cup-winning goal in double overtime of Game 7 as Detroit Red Wings beat NY Rangers, 4-3 for a 4-3 series win
Crude oil pipeline from Kirkuk, Iraq to Banias, Syria completed
"Shane", directed by George Stevens and based on the 1949 novel by Jack Schaefer, starring Alan Ladd and Jean Arthur premieres
USAF General Charles P. Cabell becomes deputy director of CIA, serves until 1962
WCOV TV channel 20 in Montgomery, Alabama (IND/CBS) begins broadcasting
Robert Kurka's "The Good Soldier Schweik", with libretto written by Abe Meeropol, premieres at the New York City Opera
1st heliport in Britain opens in London
Algiers putsch by French generals
New York Mets win their 1st game ever, after starting the season with nine losses, beating Pirates 9-1
Ranger 4, 1st US satellite to reach the Moon, launched from Cape Canaveral
Houston Colt 45s Ken Johnson becomes 1st major league pitcher to lose a 9 inning no-hitter, Reds win 1-0
New York State Theater opens
"I Can't Help Myself (Sugar Pie Honey Bunch)" single released by the Four Tops (Billboard Song of the Year 1965)
Molniya-1, 1st Soviet communications satellite launches
Soyuz 1 spacecraft launches; Vladimir Komarov becomes 1st in-flight casualty when parachute fails

Columbia University Crisis: Students for a Democratic Society and the Afro-American Society begin a non-violent, six-day strike, calling for Columbia to cut ties with the Institute for Defense Analysis. After negotiations fail, nearly 1,000 police are sent to campus, resulting in a violent clash and over 700 students arrested
First decimal coins issued in Britain, the 5 and 10 new pence, replacing the shilling and two-shilling pieces
United Methodist Church forms in Dallas, Texas
Over 1000 square miles flooded in Shantung Province, China

The Unionist Parliamentary Party votes by 28 to 22 to introduce universal adult suffrage in local government elections in Northern Ireland; the demand for 'one man, one vote' had been one of the most powerful slogans of the civil rights movement
26th Tony Awards: "Sticks and Bones" (play) & "The Two Gentlemen of Verona" (musical) win
Apollo 16 astronauts explore the moon's surface
The Sunday Times Insight Team publish their account of the events of 'Bloody Sunday'
USSR performs nuclear test at Sary Shagan USSR
ADO Den Haag soccer team forms in The Hague, Netherlands
Czech chess master, Vlastimil Hort, plays 201 games simultaneously; won-174, drew-17, lost-10
Ethiopian Red Terror: Milt workers massacre 300-500 students in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia (Red Terror 1976-78)
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Fighting in London between the Anti-Nazi League and the Metropolitan Police Special Patrol Group results in the death of protester Blair Peach
Conch Republic is established - secession of the Florida Keys from the United States of America
The 8-bit personal home computer the Sinclair ZX Spectrum is released (goes on to sell 5 million worldwide)
AIDS virus is identified as HTLV-III (Human T-lymphotropic Virus Type III)
Brooklyn College soccer team wins Nepal's All-Nepal Football Association invitational tournament 2-0
New Coke debuts; Coca-Cola announced it is changing its secret flavor formula
Disney World breaks ground for Victorian themed Grand Floridian Beach Resort (now known as Disney's Grand Floridian Resort & Spa)
28 construction workers are killed in an apartment collapse in Bridgeport, Connecticut
New Jersey Devils farm team, Maine Mariners (AHL), move to Utica New York, there known as the Utica Devils
Federal smoking ban during domestic airline flights of 2 hours or less
Greek Olympic cyclist Kanellos Kanellopoulos pedals the MIT Daedalus 88 self-powered aircraft a record 71.5 miles (115 km) from Heraklion on the island of Crete to Santorini
Karolina Szabó runs female world record for 25k (1:29:29.2) and 30k (1:47:05.6) in Budapest, Hungary
CBS' premiere of fact based "The Littlest Victims", based on Newark, N.J. physician James Oleske as the first doctor to discover AIDS in children
NFL Draft: #1 pick UCLA quarterback Troy Aikman by Dallas Cowboys
USSR grants republics the right to secede under certain conditions through a new Union Treaty
Marion Berry (former mayor of Washington, D.C.) released from prison
McDonald's opens its 1st restaurant in China, amassing over 40,000 customers on its opening day
Eritrea votes to secede from Ethiopia with 99.83% in favor
Army shoots to death 23-40 fishermen in Gonaives, Haiti
General Tire World Bowling Tournament of Champions won by Norm Duke
Howard Stern Radio Show premieres in Reno, Nevada on KRZQ 96.5 FM
Sotheby's begins a four-day auction of over 5,000 Jackie Kennedy items, amassing $34.5 million in sales
US docuseries "Forensic Files" (Medical Detectives) premieres on TLC Network
Omaria massacre in Algeria: 42 villagers are killed
NFL Draft: University of Utah quarterback Alex Smith first pick by San Francisco 49ers
YouTube's co-founder Jawed Karim uploads the first video to YouTube, "Me at the zoo" showing him in front of two elephants at the San Diego Zoo. Has over 311 million views.
Gamma ray burst (GRB) 090423 is observed for 10 seconds, the most distant object of any kind and also the oldest known object in the universe
Zach Daniels defeated Rick Michaels to become the new TNT Heavyweight Champion.
38,000 London Marathon entrants have their home and email contacts published in a data protection breach
Rangers F.C. owner, Craig Whyte, is banned for life from any involvement in Scottish football
21 people are killed during violent unrest in Xinjiang, China
28 people are killed and 70 are injured during clashes between police and Sunni Muslims in Hawija, Iraq
The French National Assembly passes an amended bill legalizing same-sex marriage
60 people are killed & 80 are injured after a train crashes in The Democratic Republic of the Congo
Armenian President Serzh Sargsyan resigns after 10 years in office after mass protests against him beginning an unconstitutional third term
Mario Abdo Benítez is elected President of Paraguay
Van deliberately driven into pedestrians in Toronto, Canada, killing 10 and injuring 13
At least 54 jade miners buried by a mudslide in Kachin state, Myanmar
Second of two major earthquakes strikes island of Samar, Philippines, magnitude 6.3, a day after magnitude 6.1 hits Luzon Island killing at least 16 people
Southampton striker Shane Long scores fastest goal in English Premier League history when he nets after 7.69 seconds in 1-1 draw at Watford
World's first malaria vaccine, giving partial protection to children, begins in Malawi by the WHO
Ecuador's COVID-19 death toll is then one of world's highest per capita after 7,600 more deaths than usual (503 official toll), in report by "The New York Times"
First findings of a frog fossil 40 million years old, found on Seymour Island, Antarctica (2015) published in "Scientific Reports"
German ChancellorAngela Merkelwarns this is "not the end phase but still just the beginning" of the COVID-19 pandemic
59th Venice Biennale contemporary art exhibition opens, with 80 participating. Main exhibition "The Milk of Dreams" curated by Cecilia Aleman.
American Simone Leigh is the first Black woman artist to win the main prize at Venice Biennale for her sculpture "Brick House"
Venice Biennale's top award, The Golden Lion (Best National Participation) awarded to UK's Sonia Boyce for her work "Feeling Her Way"
At least 100 bodies discovered in shallow graves in Shakahola forest, eastern Kenya. Believed to be members of Good News International Church, which encourages salvation through starvation.
Chevron Championship Women's Golf, The Club at Carlton Woods: Lilia Vu wins her first major beating fellow American Angel Yin on the first playoff hole
MLB Boston Red Sox outfielder Masataka Yoshida hits a solo home run and a grand slam in the eight inning in 12-5 win over the Brewers in Milwaukee
International Coral Reef Initiative releases study showing that 84% of the world's coral reefs have been impacted by an ongoing bleaching event that started in January 2023
Christian kingFerdinand III of Castileconquers the Arab-held town of Cáceres as part of the reconquest of Almohad Spain
The Order of Garter founded by English KingEdward III, the most senior order of knighthood in the British honour system; first instituted 1344, formally proclaimed 1348
Cornerstone laid for El Escorial, new Spanish royal palace commissioned byPhilip II, by architect Juan Bautista de Toledo
William Shakespeare's "The Merry Wives of Windsor" is first performed withQueen Elizabeth Iof England in attendance
PrinceFrederick Henryis appointed Stadtholder of Holland, Zeeland, Utrecht, Guelders, and Overijssel
English kingCharles IIcrowned at Westminster Abbey
Queen Anneis crowned at Westminster Abbey, London
Richard Steele's play "Tender Husband" premieres in London
Opera "Il Ré Pastore" (The Shepherd King) byWolfgang Amadeus Mozartis first produced in Salzburg
President-electGeorge Washingtonmoves into Franklin House, New York, first official residence of a US President
Russian writerFyodor Dostoevskyand members of the Petrashevsky Circle are arrested in St. Petersburg
Robert E. Leeis named commander of Virginia's Confederate forces (US Civil War)
W. S. GilbertandArthur Sullivan's comic opera "Patience" premieres at The Opera Comique, London
1st London performance ofFranz Lehár’s operetta "Frasquita" is staged
US gangster film "The Public Enemy" starringJames CagneyandJean Harlowpremieres
New York Giants pitcherCarl Hubbell's first start of season, a 3-0 win over the Boston Bees, is his 17th straight win; streak continues for league record 24 victories in a row
Ted Williamshits his 1st Major League home run while playing for the Boston Red Sox
Bob Cain of St. Louis Browns andBob Fellerof Cleveland Indians each pitch a one-hitter, as the home team wins 1-0 at Sportsman's Park in St. Louis, Missouri
NY GiantHoyt Wilhelmwins his first game in relief and hits his only home run
Hammerin'Hank Aaronhits 1st of his 755 homers
Gil Hodgeshits his 300th home run and Pee Wee Reese plays in 2,000th game, a double-milestone for Los Angeles Dodgers
SingerJudy Garlandappears in concert at Carnegie Hall, NYC; the event is recorded for a double live album and wins a Grammy Award for Album of the Year, the first by a female artist

American composer Randall Thompson marks his retirement from the Harvard faculty conducting the debut of his orchestrated arrangement of "Frostiana: Seven Country Songs" with the Harvard Glee Club and Radcliffe Choral Society singing poetry ofRobert Frost
Sirhan Sirhansentenced to death for killing US SenatorRobert F. Kennedy(later commuted to a life sentence)
Harold Pinter's play "No Man's Land" premieres in London
MLB Cincinnati Reds second basemanJoe Morganends record streak of 91 consecutive errorless games
Kareem Abdul-Jabbarscores 10 points in his last game as a Laker in a 121-117 win over Seattle SuperSonics at the LA Forum
Students in Beijing, China announce classroom boycotts as part of pro-democracy protests following the death of reformist leaderHu Yaobang
Texas Rangers'Nolan Ryanloses a no-hitter in the 9th inning against the Toronto Blue Jays
Wine merchant William Sokolin breaks a bottle of 1787 Château Margaux, possibly belonging toThomas Jefferson, worth $500,000 at the Four Seasons restaurant in New York
Björn Borgloses 6-2, 6-3 to Jordi Arrese after an 8 year lay off without practising or playing any exhibition matches
Libertarian party nominatesHoward Sternfor Governor of New York
President Clintondeclares a national day of mourning for Oklahoma City
Fatboy Slimreleases single "Weapon of Choice", music video directed by Spike Jonze starringChristopher Walkendancing
PopeJohn Paul IImeets with U.S. Catholic Church leaders at Vatican regarding sexual abuse of minors
Netherlands Prime Minister,Mark Rutte, tenders resignation, paving the way for early elections
"Star Trek Into Darkness" directed byJ. J. Abramsstarring Chris Pine and Zachary Quinto premieres in Sydney
A 1% flash crash hits the US stock market after a news agency was hacked and claimed injury toPresident Obama
West Indian cricketerChris Gaylesmashes the fastest century in history in just 30 balls
Kawhi Leonardis named the NBA Defensive Player of the Year for the first time
Loretta Lynchis confirmed by US Senate vote, 56-43, as the first African-American woman as US Attorney-General, succeedingEric Holder
Beyoncéreleases her 6th album "Lemonade" with a 1 hour film on HBO
Adam Ondrasets a new highpoint for the Project indoor sport route in Stockholm, Sweden
Marvel's "Avengers: Infinity War" directed by Anthony and Joe Russo, starringChris Evans,Robert Downey Jrand large ensemble cast premieres in Los Angeles, California
NFL Draft: LSU quarterbackJoe Burrowfirst pick by Cincinnati Bengals

US PresidentDonald Trumpsuggests studying if COVID-19 could be treated by introducing disinfectant or UV lights into a human body, during a White House press briefing; government officials and disinfectant companies quickly state doing so is not only extremely dangerous but potentially deadly

Australian PMAnthony Albanesehits back atElon Musksaying it will "do what's necessary to take on this arrogant billionaire who thinks he's above the law" as a court orders X take down terrorist attack footage
Details of ancient Greek philosopherPlato's last night and burial place revealed by deciphering a carbonized scroll from Herculaneum, using new technologies including AI
King Brian Boru of Ireland defeats Viking forces at the Battle of Clontarf, freeing Ireland from foreign control

Duke Wilhelm IV of Bavaria endorses "The German Beer Purity Law" (Reinheitsgebot) and adds to it standards for the sale of beer in Bavaria, ensuring beer is only brewed from three ingredients: water, barley, and hops
Queen Victoriaand Napoleon III turn down plans for a channel tunnel
Turkish Grand National Assembly 1st meets in Ankara, under the leadership ofMustafa Kemal Atatürkdenounces the government of Sultan Mehmed VI and announces a temporary constitution
Beijing closes all schools for two weeks because of the SARS virus
King Malcolm IV of Scotland, nicknamed Virgo "the Maiden" King of Scots (1153-65), born in Scotland (approximate dates April or May) (d. 1165)
Afonso II, 3rd King of Portugal (1211-23), born in Coimbra, Kingdom of Portugal (d. 1223)
Olivier V de Clisson, "The Butcher", French-Breton military commander (Hundred Years' War), born in Château de Clisson, Brittany (d. 1407)
Joan of France, Duchess of Berry and Queen of France (1498-1505) and catholic saint who founded several religious orders, born in Nogent-le-Roi, County of Dreux (d. 1505)
Robert Fayrfax, English Renaissance composer (Courts of Henry VII and Henry VIII), born in Deeping Gate, Lincolnshire, England (d. 1521)
Julius Caesar Scaliger, Italian-French physician and scholar (On the Subtlety of Things), born in Riva, Italy (d. 1558)
Alexander Ales [Alesius], Scottish theologian of the school of Augsburg, born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1565)
Georg Fabricius, German poet, historian and archaeologist, born in Chemnitz, Germany (d. 1571)
William Penn, English admiral and politician, father of the founder of the Province of Pennsylvania, born in St. Thomas Parish, Bristol (d. 1670)
Jan Adam Reincken, Dutch-German composer, born in Deventer, Netherlands (d. 1722)
Johan van Waveren Hudde, Dutch mathematician (Concerning Reduction of Equations) and Mayor of Amsterdam (1672-1703), born in Amsterdam (d. 1704)
Jan Commelin, Dutch botanist and director of Hortus Botanicus, Amsterdam, born in Leiden, Netherlands (d. 1692)
Andreas Kneller, German composer, born in the Free Imperial City of Lübeck, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1724)
George Anson, 1st Baron Anson, British Royal Navy officer, born in Staffordshire, England (d. 1762)
Friedrich von Hagedorn, German poet (Versuch einiger Poem), born in Hamburg, Germany (d. 1754)
Johann Friedrich Doles, German composer, pupil of J. S. Bach, born in Steinbach, Duchy of Saxe-Meiningen, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1797)
Vilna Gaon, Lithuanian rabbi, born in Sialiec, Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth (d. 1797)
Saint Gerard Majella, Catholic saint, born in Muro, Naples, Italy (d. 1755)
Samuel Wallis, English explorer (Wallis Island), born in Camelford, Cornwall, England (d. 1795)
William Williams, American merchant (signed US Declaration of Independence), born in Lebanon, Connecticut (d. 1811)
Ildephons Haas, German composer (Exultate jubilate Deo), born in Free Imperial City of Offenburg, Holy Roman Empre (d. 1791)
Félix Vicq-d'Azyr, French physician and anatomist (discover the theory of homology in biology), born in Valognes, Normandy, France (d. 1794)
Abbé Alexandre-Auguste Robineau, French composer and painter, born in Paris (d. 1828)
Alexander Reinagle, English-American composer, born in Portsmouth, England (d. 1809)
Thomas Potter Cooke, English actor, first person to portray Frankenstein on stage, born in London (d. 1864)[1]
Friedrich von Olivier, German landscape painter, born in Dessau, Germany (d. 1859)
John Romney Robinson, Irish astronomer and physicist, born in Dublin, Ireland (d. 1882)
Wei Yuan, Chinese scholar and geographer of Qing Dynasty, born in Shaoyang, China (d. 1857)
Jules J baron d'Anethan, Belgian minister of Justice, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1888)
Guillaume Nerenburger, Belgian general (Triangulatie of Belgium), born in Amsterdam (d. 1869)
Eugene-Prosper Prevost, French composer, born in Paris (d. 1872)
Thomas Wright, English antiquarian and writer, born in Ludlow, Shropshire, England (d. 1877)
Louis-Antoine Jullien, French conductor, born in Sisteron, France (d. 1860)
Frédéric Ozanam, French scholar and founder of the Society of Saint Vincent de Paul, born in Milan, Italy (d. 1853)
Stephen A. Douglas, American politician, US senator from Illinois (Lincoln-Douglas debates), born in Brandon, Vermont (d. 1861)
James Anthony Froude, English historian and novelist (The Nemesis of Faith), born in Dartington Rectory, Devon (d. 1894)
John Gill Shorter, American politician (17th Governor of Alabama), born in Monticello, Georgia (d. 1872)
Pierre Dupont, French songwriter, born in Lyon, France (d. 1870)
Abdulmejid I, 31st Sultan of Ottoman Empire, born in Istanbul, Ottoman Empire (d. 1861)
Johann Friedrich von Schulte, German Catholic lawyer, born in Winterberg, Germany (d. 1914)
Albert, King of Saxony (1873-1902), born in Dresden, Kingdom of Saxony (d. 1902)
Alfred Verwee, Flemish painter, born in Saint-Josse-ten-Noode, Belgium (d. 1895)
Edwin Markham, American poet (1st winner of American Acad of Poets Award 1937), born in Oregon City (d. 1940)
Winthrop M. Crane, American politician, 40th Governor of Massachusetts, born in Dalton, Massachusetts (d. 1920)
Granville Woods, American inventor, 1st post civil war African American mechanical and electrical engineer, invented telegraph for railways, born in Columbus, Ohio (d. 1910)[1]
Ruggero Leoncavallo, Italian opera composer and librettist (I Pagliacci; Zazà), born in Naples, Kingdom of the Two Sicilies (d. 1919)
Max Planck, German physicist, father of Quantum Physics (Planck Constant, Nobel 1918), born in Kiel, Duchy of Holstein (d. 1947)
Edmund Allenby, Viscount Allenby, British Fieldmarshal, General at Battle of Megiddo, born in Brackenhurst, England (d. 1936)
Ali-Agha Shikhlinski, Russian-Azerbaijani general, born in Kazakhli, Russian Empire (d. 1943)
Simon Abramsz, Dutch teacher and writer (For the Young Ones), born in Amsterdam (d. 1924)
Violet Gordon-Woodhouse, British harpsichordist and clavichord player, born in Harley Street, St Marylebone, London (d. 1948)
Arthur Moeller van den Bruck, German historian (The Third Reich), born in Solingen, Westphalia, German Empire (d. 1925)
Arthur Farwell, American composer, born in St Paul, Minnesota (d. 1952)
Emil Barth, German revolutionary (Social Democratic Party), born in Heidelberg (d. 1941)
Claude Carter, South African cricket spin bowler (19 Tests, 28 wickets, BB 6/50; Natal, Transvaal), born in Durban, South Africa (d. 1952)
Albert Coates, English conductor and composer (Eagle; Pickwick), born in St Petersburg, Russia (d. 1953)
Max Winders, Belgian architect (WWII), responsible for bringing the Belgian gold stock, born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1982)
Robert Imrie, American diplomat and spy, whose death in a riot in Tehran created an international incident, born in Washington D.C. (d. 1924)[1]
Georges Vanier, French Canadian soldier and 19th Governor General of Canada, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1967)
Charles Warrell, English schoolteacher and creator of the I-Spy books, born in Farmborough, Somerset (d. 1995)
Donald Tweedy, American composer, and educator, born in Danbury, Connecticut (d. 1948)
Marcel L'Herbier, French director and screenwriter (El Dorado), born in Paris (d. 1979)
Minus van Looi [Benjamin van der Voort], Flemish writer, born in Tessenderlo, Belgium (d. 1952)
Richard Huelsenbeck, German Dada writer, born in Frankenau, Hessen-Nassau (d. 1974)
Frank Borzage, American director (7th Heaven, Strange Cargo), born in Salt Lake City, Utah (d. 1962)
Basil Sydney, British actor (Hamlet, Jassy, Simba, Farmer's Wife), born in St. Osyth, Essex, England (d. 1968)
Charles "Cow Cow" Davenport, American boogie-woogie and blues musician, born in Anniston, Alabama (d. 1955)
George Renevant [Georges de Cheux], French actor (Moulin Rouge, Scotland Yard, Comrade X), born in Paris, France (d. 1969)
Ngaio Marsh, New Zealand detective writer and producer, born in Christchurch, New Zealand (d. 1982)
Margaret Kennedy, English novelist (The Constant Nymph), born in Hyde Park Gate, London (d. 1967)
John Wengraf, Austrian actor (12 to the Moon, Pride & Passion), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1974)
Lucius du Bignon Clay, American WWII general and military governor of West Germany, born in Marietta, Georgia (d. 1978)
Edwin Erich Dwinger, German writer (General Vlassov), born in Kiel, Germany (d. 1981)
Minoru Shirota, Japanese microbiologist and fouder of the Yakult Honsha beverage company, born in Tatsuoka, Nagano (d. 1982)
Ary Verhaar, Dutch composer, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1994)
Harold French, British director and actor (The Cavalier of the Streets, Trio, Quartet), born in Lambeth, London (d. 1997)
Henry Barraud, French composer, born in Bordeaux, France (d. 1997)
Joseph Green, Polish-American filmmaker, born in Łódź, Poland (d. 1996)
E.B. Ford, British ecological geneticist, born in Dalton-in-Furness, Lancashire (d. 1988)
Howard Engstrom, American computer designer (co-creator Univac computer), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1962)
Guy Simonds, Canadian army, youngest General officer, born in Bury St Edmunds, Suffolk, England (d. 1974)
Duncan Renaldo, Spanish actor (The Cisco Kid, The Gay Amigo), born in Oancea, Romania (d. 1980)
Leslie French, British actor and singer (Death in Venice; More than a Miracle), born in Bromley, Kent, England (d. 1999)
Lee Miller, American photographer (War correspondent for Vogue in WWII), born in Poughkeepsie, New York (d. 1977)
Marcel Hillaire, German actor (Take the Money and Run, Seven Thieves), born in Cologne, Germany (d. 1988)
Myron Waldman, American animator (Fleischer Studio), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2006)
(Frederick) "Erick" Hawkins, American modern ballet dancer, and choreographer (Martha Graham Dance Company, 1939-51; Erick Hawkins Dance Company, 1951-94), born in Trinidad, Colorado (d. 1994)
Thomas Padmore, British senior civil servant, born in Sheffield, England (d. 1996)
Józef Cyrankiewicz, Polish socialist, and later communist, politician, (Premier of Polish People's Republic, 1947-52 & 1954-70), born in Tarnow, Austria-Hungary (d. 1989)
Ronald Neame, British director (1st Monday in October; The Poseidon Adventure), born in London, England (d. 2010)
Simone Simon, French actress (All Money Can Buy, Ladies in Love), born in Marseille, Bouches-du-Rhône, France (d. 2005)
Jan Meyerowitz, German-American composer, born in Breslau, Germany (d. 1998)
John Hubbard, American actor (Don't Call Me Charlie, The Tall T, One Million B.C.), born in East Chicago, Indiana (d. 1988)
Mitsu Suzuki, Japanese-American poet, tea teacher and wife of San Francisco Zen Center founder Shunryi Suzuki, born in Japan (d. 2016)
Arnold Hall, British aeronautical engineer, born in Liverpool, England (d. 2000)
Bud Wilkinson, American college football coach (Oklahoma), born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 1994)
Jacob Kistemaker, Dutch nuclear physicist (ultra centrifuge), born in Kolhorn, Netherlands (d. 2010)
Anthony Craxton, British TV producer, born in London (d. 1966)
Gordon Hirabayshi, American civil rights activist and WWII internment opponent (Hirabayshi v. United States), born in Seattle, Washington (d. 2012)
Maurice Druon, French writer and journalist (Prix Goncourt), born in Paris (d. 2009)
Arthur K. Watson, American businessman (IBM), and US Ambassador to France (1970-72), born in Summit, New Jersey (d. 1974)[1]

Bülent Arel, Turkish-American contemporary classical and electronic music composer (Stereo Electronic Music No.1), educator (Yale, 1961-70; SUNY Purchase, 1971-89), and visual artist, born in Istanbul, Turkey (d. 1990)
Dorian Leigh, American supermodel and icon of the fashion industry, born in San Antonio, Texas (d. 2008)
Oleg Penkovsky, Soviet military intelligence (GRU), born in Vladikavkaz, Russia (d. 1963)
Tālivaldis Ķeniņš, Latvian-Canadian composer, organist, and pedagogue, born in Liepāja, Latvia (d. 2008)
Louis Barron, American composer and electronic music pioneer (with his wife Bebe - Forbidden Planet), born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 1989)
Janet Blair [Martha Janet Lafferty], American singer and actress (Leave it to the Girls, Smith Family), born in Altoona, Pennsylvania (d. 2007)
Boy Ecury [Segundo Jorge Adelberto Ecury], member of the Dutch Resistance in World War II, born in Oranjestad, Aruba (d. 1944)
Diarmuid Downs, British automotive engineer, born in London (d. 2014)
Antonino Rocca, Italian-Argentine professional wrestler, born in Treviso, Veneto, Italy (d. 1977)
